expect: 30.7% DECAYED exceeds healthy org forgetting (15-25%). Prediction: >=40% of DECAYED items are pathological (still-needed knowledge lost from active engagement).
actual: 30.0% pathological rate — BELOW the >=40% prediction threshold. 70% of DECAYED lessons are functionally decayed (redundant, era-specific, or domain-irrelevant). The 30.7% overall DECAYED rate is epistemically HEALTHY when adjusted for functional decay.
diff: Prediction FALSIFIED. Pathological rate 30.0% < 40% threshold. Effective pathological decay = 30.7% * 30.0% = 9.2% of total corpus. This falls WITHIN the healthy range for organizational declarative knowledge (Walsh & Ungson: up to 30%).

method.approach: Stratified sampling of 30 DECAYED lessons (10 high-citation >=5, 10 medium 2-4, 10 low 1) with manual classification into functional-decay vs pathological-decay based on content reading, citation analysis, and current relevance assessment.

functional_decay.redundant: Superseded by newer lesson with better data or absorbed into successor work
functional_decay.era_specific: Finding tied to a specific point in time (tool counts, file sizes, audit snapshots) with no reusable insight beyond what successors capture
functional_decay.domain_irrelevant: About external software, dead-end research direction, or domain no longer actively pursued

pathological_decay.still_needed: Insight is still operationally relevant, has high citation in-degree indicating wide downstream dependency, and no successor lesson captures the same finding

results.note: Effective pathological = overall DECAYED rate (30.4%) * pathological fraction (30.0%) = 9.2% of total corpus

ebbinghaus_forgetting_curve.applicability: LOW — individual episodic memory, not organizational knowledge system with persistent storage
ebbinghaus_forgetting_curve.comparison: Swarm's 30.4% DECAYED after 50+ sessions without citation is far BETTER than Ebbinghaus predicts, but the analogy is weak because lessons are stored in files, not biological memory

organizational_forgetting_walsh_ungson_1.baseline: 10-20% procedural, up to 30% declarative knowledge loss is healthy
organizational_forgetting_walsh_ungson_1.applicability: MEDIUM — closest analog; swarm lessons are mostly declarative knowledge
organizational_forgetting_walsh_ungson_1.comparison: Effective pathological rate 9.2% is WITHIN healthy range. Total DECAYED 30.4% appears high but 70% is functional (redundant/obsolete), bringing real loss to ~9%

organizational_learning_argote_2013.baseline: Knowledge depreciation rates of 10-30% per year in manufacturing; higher in dynamic environments
organizational_learning_argote_2013.applicability: MEDIUM — swarm operates in a highly dynamic self-modifying environment
organizational_learning_argote_2013.comparison: 9.2% effective loss rate is at the LOW end of Argote's range, suggesting the swarm retains knowledge well relative to its rate of change

transactive_memory_wegner_1987.baseline: 40-60% of group knowledge is distributed (stored in other members, not centrally)
transactive_memory_wegner_1987.applicability: LOW — swarm has a single shared memory, not distributed across members
transactive_memory_wegner_1987.comparison: Not directly applicable. However, DECAYED lessons that are 'redundant' (30% of sample) function like distributed knowledge — the insight exists elsewhere in the corpus

pathological_items.title: Predictive coding operational — loop closure drives quality
pathological_items.why_pathological: Foundational EAD measurement (+39.8pp). 16 downstream citations. No successor captures this specific effect size.

pathological_items.title: FALSE ABANDONED — artifact-existence guard prevents misclassification
pathological_items.why_pathological: Documents commit-by-proxy misclassification mechanism. Guard is still in close_lane.py code.

pathological_items.title: Grounding floor theorem — 1/3 automatic compliance
pathological_items.why_pathological: Structural minimum for grounding fields. Still the baseline for F-HIS1 measurements.

pathological_items.title: One-shot DOMEX confirmed: 100% MERGED vs 8% baseline
pathological_items.why_pathological: Canonical evidence for expert dispatch norms. 10 citations. No replacement measurement.

pathological_items.title: Knowledge decays at mechanism layer not principle layer
pathological_items.why_pathological: Meta-epistemological finding directly relevant to THIS experiment. 14 citations. Core quality insight.

pathological_items.title: WIP spread hurts, synthesis spread helps — opposite signs
pathological_items.why_pathological: Fundamental work strategy insight. WIP vs synthesis distinction still guides dispatch.

pathological_items.title: Federated convergence tags lost to commit-by-proxy
pathological_items.why_pathological: Documents real failure mode for unstaged working-tree changes in concurrent sessions.

pathological_items.title: Genesis-era 40% non-current, verification-confidence paradox
pathological_items.why_pathological: Epistemological finding about trust in legacy labels. Relevant to any audit of old lessons.

pathological_items.title: History integrity — experiment outcomes 39% gap
pathological_items.why_pathological: Identifies a 61% experiment outcome completeness gap that still persists.

key_finding: Citation in-degree is a strong predictor of pathological decay: 50% pathological at in-degree>=5, 30% at 2-4, 10% at 1. High-citation DECAYED items (avg in-degree 7.1 vs 3.2 for functional) represent load-bearing knowledge with wide downstream dependency. The swarm should prioritize reviving DECAYED items with in-degree >= 5.

verdict.overall_health: HEALTHY — 30.4% DECAYED rate is epistemically healthy when 70% is functional decay. Effective pathological loss rate of 9.2% falls within Walsh & Ungson's healthy organizational forgetting range (10-30% declarative). The swarm's knowledge system is functioning as a healthy organizational memory, not a pathological one.
verdict.actionable_risk: The 9 pathological items (especially L-646 in_degree=16, L-633 in_degree=14, L-444 in_degree=10) should be revived — they are load-bearing knowledge nodes with no active successors. A targeted revival of DECAYED items with in_degree >= 5 would address 56% of pathological decay.
verdict.citation_in_degree_as_triage: Proposed heuristic: DECAYED + in_degree >= 5 = revival candidate. This would flag ~128 items (high stratum) for review, of which ~50% are expected pathological based on our sample.
